title: PyBot Loops
description: Use for loops and nested loops to control PyBot. Progress from single commands to walking a full square perimeter with nested loops.

steps.title: Meet PyBot!
steps.content:
Meet PyBot! 🤖

PyBot is a robot you control using Python. Your job is to guide it to special tiles on the grid and light them up.

You have four commands:

walk_forward() — move one tile forward (only if it's the same height)
turn_right() — turn 90° clockwise
jump() — move one tile forward, but only if the next tile is exactly 1 level higher or 1 level lower.
turn_left() — turn 90° anti-clockwise
light() — light up the tile you're standing on

⚠️ The level is complete when all glowing tiles have been lit. Each level shows how many lines of code you need for Gold 🥇, Silver 🥈, or Bronze 🥉 medals.

Click Next Step to try your first level!

steps.title: Level 1 — Your First Light
steps.content:
Level 1 — Your First Light

PyBot starts on the left. The glowing tile is at the far right. Walk forward to reach it, then call light().

Try writing the code yourself — use walk_forward() once for each tile, then light() at the end.

💡 Hint: You need to walk 4 tiles then light up. That's 4 walk_forward() calls then one light().

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Writing Less Code with Loops
steps.content:
That was a lot of typing! 🤔

If the light was 20 tiles away, you'd need to write walk_forward() twenty times. That gets boring fast!

A for loop tells Python to repeat something:

for i in range(4):
    walk_forward()

This does exactly the same as writing walk_forward() four times — but in just two lines!

💡 Remember the indent! The walk_forward() line is pushed in with spaces. This tells Python it belongs inside the loop.

On the next level, try to get the Gold medal by using a loop.

Watch the loop in action below — PyBot walks 5 tiles and lights up, over and over:

steps.title: Level 2 — Use a Loop!
steps.content:
Level 2 — Use a Loop!

This time the light is 5 tiles away. You could write walk_forward() five times — but to get Gold you'll need to use a loop!

Gold requires 3 lines or fewer. Can you do it?

💡 Hint: for i in range(5): then walk_forward() indented below, then light() after the loop.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Turning
steps.content:
Turning ↪

PyBot can also turn! turn_right() turns 90° clockwise. turn_left() turns 90° the other way.

walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
walk_forward()

This walks two tiles forward, then turns right, then walks one more tile in the new direction.

⚠️ turning does not move PyBot — it just changes the direction they're facing. You still need walk_forward() after a turn.

Watch PyBot demonstrate below — it walks two tiles, turns right, walks two more, then lights up:

steps.title: Level 3 — Turn the Corner
steps.content:
Level 3 — Turn the Corner

There are now two lights to reach. One is to the right, one is below it. You'll need to walk, then turn, then walk again.

The route makes an L shape. Think about where to turn and when to use light().

💡 Hint: Walk right to the first light. Then turn_right() to face downward. Walk down to the second light.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Loop + Turn Pattern
steps.content:
Combining loops and turns

If you need to walk several steps and then turn, you can put the walking in a loop:

for i in range(4):
    walk_forward()
turn_right()
for i in range(3):
    walk_forward()

This walks forward 4 times, turns right, then walks 3 more times — in just 7 lines instead of 9!

On the next level, use this technique to reach all the lights.

Here it is in action — two loops with a turn in between:

steps.title: Level 4 — L Shape with Loops
steps.content:
Level 4 — L Shape with Loops

A longer L-shape this time. Two lights are waiting at the bottom-right corner. Use two for loops — one for each straight section — to reach them efficiently.

💡 Hint: Walk 4 right using a loop, then turn_right(), then walk 2 down using a loop. Light up both tiles at the end.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Going in a Square
steps.content:
Can PyBot walk in a square? □

A square has 4 sides. Each side is the same length. After each side, PyBot turns right.

Here's how it looks without a loop:

# Side 1
walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
# Side 2
walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
# Side 3
...and so on

That's very repetitive! Look at the pattern: walk twice, turn right — and you do that same thing 4 times.

That means you can use a loop! Try it on the next level.

Watch PyBot walk three sides of a square — each side is walk, walk, light, turn:

steps.title: Level 5 — Walk in a Square
steps.content:
Level 5 — Walk in a Square

There are lights at three corners of a square. Walk around the square and light them all up.

Use a loop that repeats 3 times (one for each corner with a light): walk forward twice, light up, then turn right.

💡 Hint: Try: for side in range(3): walk_forward(), walk_forward(), light(), turn_right()

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Nested Loops
steps.content:
A loop inside a loop!

What if you need to walk 3 steps on each side of a square? You could write walk_forward() three times inside the loop — but there's an even better way.

You can put a loop inside another loop. This is called a nested loop:

for side in range(4):
    for step in range(3):
        walk_forward()
    turn_right()

Let's trace through it:

  • The outer loop runs 4 times (one per side of the square)
  • Each time, the inner loop runs 3 times (3 steps forward)
  • Then PyBot turns right, ready for the next side

💡 Count the indents! The inner loop is indented once (inside outer), and walk_forward() is indented twice (inside inner). turn_right() is indented once — inside outer but after inner.

Watch the nested loop in action — the inner loop handles the steps, the outer loop handles the sides:

steps.title: Level 6 — Nested Loop Square
steps.content:
Level 6 — Nested Loop Square

PyBot starts at the bottom-left corner facing up. There are lights at three other corners. Use a nested loop to walk 3 steps on each of the 3 lit sides.

The pattern is: walk 3 steps, light up, turn right — do that 3 times.

💡 Hint: Outer loop 3 times. Inner loop 3 times with walk_forward(). Then light() and turn_right() inside the outer loop.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 1 — Bigger Square
steps.content:
Challenge 1 — Bigger Square

Challenge 1 🏆

A bigger grid this time! Three lights are waiting at corners of a 5×5 square. Can you work out how many steps each side needs?

Try to get Gold by keeping your solution to 4 lines or fewer.

🤔 Think first: the grid is 5×5. PyBot starts in a corner. Each side has 4 steps to the next corner. How do you change the inner loop?

💡 Hint: Change the inner range() to match the new step count. The rest of the structure stays the same!

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 2 — Full Perimeter
steps.content:
Challenge 2 — Full Perimeter

Challenge 2 🔥

Now light up the entire perimeter of a 4×4 grid — all 4 corners need lighting. PyBot starts at the bottom-left facing up.

This time you need to go around all 4 sides — including coming back to where you started. The start tile also needs a light!

💡 Start with light() before the loop, then loop 4 times.

💡 Hint: Light the starting tile first. Then loop 4 times: walk 3 steps, light, turn right.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Jumping!
steps.content:
A new command: jump()! 🚀

walk_forward() only works on flat ground. If there's a ledge in the way, PyBot needs to jump!

jump() — jumps forward one tile if the height is one higher or lower
walk_forward() — walks forward one tile if the height is exactly the same 
walk_forward()  # flat ground
walk_forward()  # still flat
jump()          # up one level
light()         # light up!

⚠️ jump() does not work on flat ground — you must use walk_forward() on same-height tiles. Getting them confused is the most common mistake!

Watch PyBot demonstrate below:

steps.title: Level 7 — Your First Jump
steps.content:
Level 7 — Your First Jump

There's a raised platform ahead with a light on it. Walk up to the base, then jump up to reach the light.

Remember: walk_forward() for flat ground, jump() for a ledge.

💡 Hint: Walk to the base of the step, then use jump() to get up. Then light().

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Staircases and Loops
steps.content:
Jumping up a staircase

When every tile is one step higher than the last, PyBot needs to jump() every time. That's a great opportunity for a loop!

for i in range(4):
    jump()
light()

This jumps up 4 steps then lights up at the top — only 3 lines!

The same pattern works going downhill too — jump() works whether the next tile is higher or lower by one.

Watch PyBot demonstrate below:

steps.title: Level 8 — Up and Over
steps.content:
Level 8 — Up and Over

PyBot needs to climb a staircase, reach the peak, and come back down to the light. Every step is one tile higher or lower — that means every move is a jump!

Use a loop to make this clean. You'll need 4 jumps in total to get from bottom-left to bottom-right.

💡 Hint: All 4 moves are jumps. Can you do it in 3 lines using for i in range(4): jump()?

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Mixing walk and jump
steps.content:
Walk and jump together

Most real levels mix flat ground and ledges. You'll need to choose walk_forward() or jump() for each tile depending on the height.

for i in range(2):
    walk_forward()  # flat section
    jump()          # step up
light()

This pattern — walk then jump, twice — handles a gently rising staircase where the ground rises every other tile.

Watch PyBot demonstrate below:

steps.title: Level 9 — Step by Step
steps.content:
Level 9 — Step by Step

The terrain rises gradually — two flat sections separated by steps. You'll need to mix walk_forward() and jump() to get there.

Look at the heights carefully: flat, flat, step up, flat, step up. Can you spot the repeating pattern and use a loop?

💡 Hint: The pattern repeats twice: walk on the flat, then jump up the step. Try for i in range(2): with both commands inside.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 3 — Big Staircase
steps.content:
Challenge 3 — Big Staircase 🏆

A 7-tile staircase — up 3 steps to the peak, then back down 3 to the light. Every move is a jump.

You need 6 jumps in total. Can you do it in 5 lines or fewer?

🤔 Think: 3 jumps up, then 3 jumps down. That's two separate loops — or one loop of 6.

💡 Hint: Two loops of 3, or one loop of 6. Either works — but can you get Gold?

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 4 — Two Platforms
steps.content:
Challenge 4 — Two Platforms 🔥

Two raised platforms with a light on each. You'll need to jump up, light, jump down, walk across, jump up again, and light again.

There's a repeating pattern here — look carefully and you might be able to use a loop.

💡 Hint: The pattern: jump up, light, jump down, walk — repeats. Try a loop that does those 4 things twice.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 5 — Jump and Turn
steps.content:
Challenge 5 — Jump and Turn 💪

This level combines jumping and turning. Walk forward, climb up, turn right, then jump down to the light.

Read the terrain carefully — some tiles need walk_forward(), some need jump().

💡 Hint: Walk 2 tiles flat, jump up, turn right, walk 2 more, jump down to the light.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 1 — Long Staircase
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 1 — Long Staircase

A long uphill staircase. Every tile is one higher than the last. How few lines can you use?

💡 Hint: Use for i in range(6): jump() then light().

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 2 — Square with Heights
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 2 — Square with Heights

A square path — but the corners are raised! Walk the flat sections, jump to climb the corners.

The grid is 4×4. The corner tiles are height 2, everything else is height 1.

💡 Hint: Walk forward 1 flat tile, jump UP onto the corner, turn right, then jump DOWN onto the next edge. Put this in a loop!

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 3 — Zigzag
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 3 — Zigzag

A zigzag path of alternating heights. Every other tile is a step up. Use a loop to handle the repeating pattern.

💡 Hint: The pattern is: walk, jump, walk, jump… use for i in range(4): with walk then jump inside.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 4 — Valley
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 4 — Valley

PyBot starts high, drops into a valley, walks along the bottom, then climbs back up to the light.

💡 Hint: Jump down, walk across the valley floor, jump back up to the light. How many of each do you need?

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 5 — Full Square (5×5)
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 5 — Full Square (5×5)

Light all four corners of a 5×5 square. Start at the bottom-left corner, go around all 4 sides.

You need to light the starting tile first, then loop 4 times. How many steps per side?

💡 Hint: light() first, then outer loop 4 times: inner loop 4 steps, light(), turn_right().

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 6 — Pyramid
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 6 — Pyramid

Climb a symmetrical pyramid — up 3 then down 3 — then light the tile at the base.

The tricky part: the middle tile at the peak is flat (same height as the tile you just jumped to), so you need a walk_forward() at the top.

💡 Hint: Jump up 3 times, walk forward across the flat peak, jump down 3 times, light.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 7 — Lights Along a Loop
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 7 — Lights Along a Loop

Walk a 4×4 square path, lighting up every corner as you go — including where you start.

Try to do it with the most efficient code possible. Can you still get Gold?

💡 Hint: Light the start first. Then loop 4 times: 3 steps, light, turn.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Extra Challenge 8 — Jump the Gap
steps.content:
Extra Challenge 8 — Jump the Gap

The terrain drops down then rises back up — you need to jump into the valley and jump back out.

Walk to the edge, jump down, walk the valley floor, jump up, reach the light.

💡 Hint: Check each height: where do you walk, where do you jump?

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Endless Mode
steps.content:
Endless Mode ∞

Keep practising! Each level gets a little harder. Solve it and a new one appears. Try to get Gold on every one!

Streak: 0
Best: 0
